text: The laparoscopic total proctocolectomy begins with the inferior to superior mobilization of the right colon. The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the root of the small bowel mesentery, progressively mobilizing the right colon until the second part of the duodenum is visualized. A piece of gauze is placed near the duodenum for future identification.

The surgeon tentatively elevates the ileocolic pedicle and incises the peritoneum below it, revealing the posterior dissection. The pedicle is meticulously dissected down to its origin, stripped of lymphatics and fibrofatty tissue, then clipped and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon in a medial to lateral fashion. The peritoneum is incised along the posterior surface of the root of the transverse mesocolon, revealing the middle colic vessels. Using cautery dissection, the fibrofatty tissue and lymphatics are carefully dissected to expose the pedicles, showing an early division of the middle colic artery into right and left branches. A gauze piece is placed for future identification.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the line of torque to take down the lateral attachments of the right colon. The gastrocolic omentum is divided using a harmonic scalpel, fully mobilizing the colon from the stomach.

The surgeon dissects along the root of the transverse colon, encountering the previously established posterior dissection. A visible window opens in the tissue, and the dissection continues towards the hepatic flexure, where the peritoneal attachments are taken down to fully mobilize the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon dissects the anterior portion of the middle colic vessels, using the previously placed gauze piece for identification. Each pedicle is individually clipped and divided, and the gauze piece is subsequently removed.

The surgeon begins rectal mobilization by dissecting the inferior mesenteric vein adjacent to the DJ flexure. The vein is elevated, and a plane is developed posterior to it. The vein is then dissected, clipped, and divided. Rectal mobilization continues at the sacral promontory.

The surgeon incises the right leaf of the rectum dysentery just inferior to the inferior mesenteric artery, performing medial to lateral mobilization. The pedicle is dissected down to its origin from the aorta, with all fibro-fatty tissue removed, before being clipped and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon in a medial to lateral fashion, taking down the lateral attachments. With good medial traction, the peritoneal fold is dissected, and the splenic flexure is completely mobilized using monopolar cautery.

The surgeon divides the left portion of the transverse mesocolon along the inferior border of the pancreas, completing the mobilization of the abdominal colon. Rectal dissection begins along the right leaf of the rectal knee sentry, extending the peritoneal incision down to the floor.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the right hypogastric nerves while continuing dissection medial to them in the avascular presacral plane. The rectum is mobilized posteriorly down to the pelvic floor, and a prominent middle rectal vessel is clipped and divided.

The surgeon incises the anterior peritoneal fold and performs anterior dissection posterior to Denonvilliers fascia, mobilizing the rectum near the seminal vesicles. Dissection continues around the right lateral side, connecting anterior and posterior planes to mobilize the rectum on the right side. Similarly, the left side is dissected posteriorly, meeting the anterior planes, resulting in circumferential mobilization of the rectum until the levator muscles are visible.
The surgeon completes the circumferential mobilization of the rectum, extending the dissection down to the anorectal junction, ensuring the rectum is fully freed from surrounding tissues.

In the perineal region, a lone star retractor is applied to evert the anal canal. The anal canal is infiltrated with a dilute adrenaline solution, followed by an incision along the dentate line. A complete anal mucosectomy is performed, and the mucosal tube is sewn shut with a gauze piece placed over it.

The surgeon divides the rectum at the anorectal junction, revealing the gauze piece previously placed in the anal canal. As the anterior half is divided, the gauze is removed to expose the mucosal tube. With traction, the muscular core of the rectum is visible, extending into the anal canal to form the internal sphincter. The anorectal junction is completely transected, freeing the specimen.

The surgeon exteriorizes the specimen through an Alexis wound port and constructs a standard J pouch using a linear stapler. The redundant mesentery is incised, and the mesenteric peritoneum is scored to ensure adequate pouch reach. The pouch is then placed into the abdomen, and a proline suture is used to deliver the pouch into the anal canal, where the apex of the pouch is opened.
The surgeon performs an interrupted hand-sewn ileal pouch anastomosis using 3-0 Vicryl sutures, completing the colectomy procedure.
